title: Google's targeted ads & privacy
summary: How do people feel about Google's use of their personal information for personalized ads, particularly regarding privacy concerns?
showNotes: Part of the Supernova podcast series where Master of Media Design students investigate algorithms embedded in our everyday lives.
transcript:
Welcome to SuperNova – Algorithms Beyond Us! Today, we explore Google's data collection and personalized ads. Have you ever wondered how your data is used and why it matters? Just a quick heads-up before we start: some clips might sound a bit different because of the recording setup. And keep in mind, each perspective comes from a unique place—we've talked to people in their 20s, 30s, and 50s. Let's dive in and explore their thoughts and experiences. Google's personalized ads are everywhere in our daily online lives. But how do people really feel about them? Do they even know how their data is being used?

So, I searched for something like a camping pillow on a site like 11st or Coupang. After searching, not long after, I visited another webpage, and there was a camping pillow ad. That's when I realized that they not only know what I search on Google but also track what I search on other sites.

If you go into your Google account, there's some way you can find out what they know about you. So your age, your status—if you're single or have a cat—or even where you work or live. When I checked, they knew I was a student.

People usually start to notice how much data is being collected when they see personalized ads. It can feel pretty surprising and even a bit uncomfortable. In Subprime Attention Crisis, Tim Hwang points out that the digital ad industry often exaggerates how effective ads really are. Platforms gather tons of user data, but the actual results don't always live up to the hype, which raises big questions about privacy and trust.

Your personal information makes you human. But when companies know everything about you without your consent, it feels dehumanizing and scary.

Well, I feel like it's part of the game since you're using free tools. I also feel pressured when ads keep showing me the same items I checked before—it's intrusive.

Samsung's terms and conditions even say they can access your phone's camera anytime to see what you're doing or use the microphone to listen to what you're saying—without your explicit permission. But let's be honest, most people don't read those terms, right? That's why it became such a big issue a while back.

People have different reactions to targeted ads, but one thing's clear: the lack of transparency makes people uncomfortable and less trusting.

Yeah, I'm always on incognito mode on my phone. I also use ad blockers on my computer, but I couldn't find a way to do it on my phone.

I try to stay disconnected from Google while browsing to avoid being tracked.

Nowadays, when an app on my iPhone asks to track my info, the phone lets me decide if I want to allow it or not. I almost always choose 'no' to stop the tracking.

Each age group looks at it differently: the 20s user is all about privacy and takes action to protect it, the 30s user tries to balance privacy with convenience, and the 50s user uses practical tools to avoid tracking. Altogether, it shows how much we need more transparency and better privacy protections. What about you? Do you feel like you can protect your privacy, or does it feel like you're stuck in the system? Is it worth giving up some privacy for convenience, or should companies do more to protect us? In the end, talking about how our data is used and staying aware are the first steps to making companies do better.

Thanks for listening to SuperNova! See you next time for more insights into the digital world!
